data.title: Evergrande’s iconic Canadian hotel to be sold after default
data.description:
China Evergrande Group’s famous log-cabin hotel in Quebec, Canada, has been placed in receivership after the defaulted real estate developer failed to make payments on its debt.
Fairmont Le Chateau Montebello, which has hosted luminaries including Grace Kelly and Margaret Thatcher, would be put up for sale by PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP, the court-appointed receiver, according to documents filed this week on the accounting firm’s website.
Evergrande’s collapse was by far the biggest in a property crisis that dragged down China’s economic growth for years and led to a record spate of distress among developers. The company, which first defaulted in 2021, was once the country’s largest developer by sales.
The firm’s insolvent subsidiary, Millennium Golden Jiachen Hotel Holdings, owes C$58 million (US$41 million) to creditors, including C$11 million to an affiliate of Desjardins Group. The Canadian lender filed a motion to put the hotel up for sale, according to court documents.
The 95-year-old hotel will remain open during the sale process. Billing itself as the world’s largest log cabin, the resort opened as a private club in the depths of the Great Depression and is now managed by Accor’s Fairmont Hotels and Resorts.
The hotel, with more than 211 rooms and suites, is located 134km west of Montreal and includes an 18-hole golf course, a spa and a conference centre. Evergrande bought it in 2014 from Oxford Properties Group, a unit of the Omers pension plan.
Quebec’s Superior Court appointed Philippe Jordan, a partner with PwC, to act as receiver earlier this month. The insolvency process was first reported by the Journal de Montreal.
“We are currently taking the necessary steps to contact stakeholders, including Millennium and Accor,” Jordan said in a phone interview. “Ultimately, the action plan will include putting the hotel up for sale.”
Last week, liquidators for the delisted China Evergrande Group asked “selected bidders” to submit offers by the end of the month for a property-management unit they have been seeking to sell, according to a filing with the Hong Kong stock exchange.
The deadline for the offer, as well as identifying bidders for China Evergrande’s stake in Evergrande Property Services (EPS), came as creditors sought to recover assets from the Guangzhou-based developer, which collapsed under the weight of more than US$300 billion in liabilities.

data.title: Beijing vows ‘zero tolerance’ ahead of Legco poll as Hong Kong’s ICAC charges 3
data.description:
Beijing’s national security arm in Hong Kong has pledged to adopt a “zero tolerance” approach in safeguarding next month’s Legislative Council election, as the city’s anti-corruption agency charged three people for allegedly inciting others to boycott the poll.
The Independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) charged three individuals for allegedly reposting online messages that urged others not to vote and, on Thursday, issued arrest warrants for two men suspected of making such posts.
The law enforcement action coincided with a renewed warning from the Office for Safeguarding National Security against any attempts to disrupt the December 7 election, issued on the same day.
“Some anti-China forces and external forces have not given up their evil intentions and continued to incite others – openly and covertly – to boycott the election,” a spokesman of the Office for Safeguarding National Security said.
He accused these forces of spreading rumours and disrupting electoral order under the guise of “democracy and freedom”, with a “true aim” of dragging Hong Kong back into political chaos.
The Beijing office noted that the city’s law enforcement and judicial authorities had taken firm action against any attempts to interfere with the election.
“The Office for Safeguarding National Security will adopt a ‘zero tolerance’ approach in safeguarding election security and maintaining election order,” the spokesman said, adding that it would also resolutely support local authorities in enforcing the national security law and other legislation to ensure the smooth running of the coming poll.

The three people charged by the ICAC – two men and a woman, aged 55 to 61 – each face one count of engaging in illegal conduct to incite another person not to vote by activity in public during an election period, under the Elections (Corrupt and Illegal Conduct) Ordinance. They were due to appear at West Kowloon Court on Thursday afternoon.
Separately, the ICAC has issued arrest warrants for self-exiled activist Alan Keung Ka-wai and Tong Wai-hung, accusing them of publishing social media posts urging others not to vote.
Keung is also separately wanted for suspected subversion under the national security law since July, with a HK$200,000 (US$25,676) bounty on his head.
Both central and local authorities have repeatedly warned against any attempts to disrupt the Legco election, the second to be held under Beijing’s “patriots-only” electoral overhaul.
On Tuesday, national security police arrested a 68-year-old man on suspicion of posting social media messages encouraging others not to vote or to cast invalid ballots in next month’s election.

data.title: Huge security operation in place for Africa’s first G20 summit
data.description:
South African police and army units held a parade on Wednesday involving helicopters, K-9 dog units and officers on motorcycles in a show of force ahead of expected protests around this weekend’s Group of 20 world leaders summit in Johannesburg.
South Africa has deployed 3,500 extra police officers and put the army on standby under its National Joint Operational and Intelligence Structure - a body that brings police, army and intelligence services together under one command to provide security for major events.
Deputy national commissioner for policing Lieutenant General Tebello Mosikili told reporters this week that authorities were expecting protests in Johannesburg and other major South African cities.
“We will allow that right (to protest) to be exercised,” she said. “But within the proper directives and proper confines of the law.”

South African police said they have designated specific areas for protesters to gather near the summit venue, an exhibition centre next to the country’s biggest soccer stadium. The head of Airports Company South Africa, which runs the main international airports, said it had set up “speakers’ corners” in airports where protesters would be “kindly” taken by security if they stage demonstrations when world leaders arrive.
The two-day summit opens on Saturday and is expected to attract leaders and top diplomats from more than 40 countries as well as global institutions like the United Nations, the World Bank, the International Monetary Fund and the World Trade Organization.
Demonstrations were expected from anti-capitalists, climate activists, women’s rights campaigners, anti-migrant groups and others, some of which are raising South Africa’s own problems with poverty and inequality.
A trade union representing members of South Africa’s Afrikaner white minority has already stoked controversy by putting up billboards around Johannesburg that say: “Welcome to the most RACE-REGULATED country in the world”. One of the boards was taken down by city authorities, prompting the Afrikaner trade union, Solidarity, to threaten legal action.
The billboards were in reference to South Africa’s affirmative action laws that advance opportunities for black people and have become part of a diplomatic fallout between South Africa and the United States.
US President Donald Trump would not attend the G20 summit in a boycott over his claims that South Africa’s black-led government was pursuing racist, anti-white policies and violently persecuting its Afrikaner minority. Trump’s claims have been widely rejected as baseless, but the US government boycott threatened to undermine the first G20 summit in Africa.

Other groups hoped to use the opportunity to draw attention to a myriad of issues.
The Women for Change advocacy group was calling for a national shutdown on Friday, the eve of the summit. It was asking women to boycott work on the day in protest at South Africa’s extremely high rates of violence against women and femicide.
“Because until South Africa stops burying a woman every 2.5 hours, the G20 cannot speak of growth and progress,” Women for Change said.
A South African anti-immigration group will protest against the joblessness and poverty in the country, its leader said, with South Africa’s 31 per cent unemployment rate one of the highest in the world.
A coalition of groups protesting against climate change and wealth inequality have organised an alternative summit in another part of Johannesburg starting on Thursday, saying the G20 gathering is “for the rich”.
Authorities in Johannesburg have also embarked on a major clear-up and repair operation ahead of the summit to tackle some of the broken-down infrastructure that plagues South Africa’s biggest city.

South African President Cyril Ramaphosa joined the effort last week, when he threw on a pair of green overalls and helped with some of the work in the township of Soweto just a few miles from where the summit will be held.
The multimillion-dollar operation for a two-day meeting of politicians has been viewed with cynicism by many in Johannesburg who have become accustomed to broken street lights, cracked and potholed roads and deteriorating services that lead to water and electricity blackouts.
“Us hosting this G20, I don’t really think it’s going to be beneficial for South Africans, for the ordinary South Africans. It’s just a way to waste money, if I can say,” Johannesburg resident Lerato Lelusa said.

data.title: Sandy Lam, Placido Domingo, Zhou Shen: 5 China Greater Bay Area events in December 2025
data.description:
From major pop stars to classical music icons, the Greater Bay Area (GBA) will play host to some exciting musical acts in December, including a beloved Hong Kong singer, who will perform a New Year’s Eve show.
Read on to see what is coming up in Shenzhen and Guangzhou.
1. Concerto di Placido Domingo: Guangzhou

Legendary opera singer Placido Domingo is returning to the GBA after his October 2024 Macau concert.
This time, he will perform at the Zaha Hadid-designed Guangzhou Opera House, supported by the Cuban-American soprano Monica Conesa and accompanied by the Guangzhou Symphony Orchestra.
The programme is divided into two halves: the first will feature arias from Italian classics such as Tosca and Il trovatore, while the second will be made up of more modern pieces.
The Spaniard, who has won various Grammy and Latin Grammy Awards, has sung in over 4,000 performances throughout his six-decade-long career.
Date: December 2
Venue: Guangzhou Opera House
How to get there: Guangzhou Metro Line 3 to Canton Tower Station, or Line 3 or 5 to Zhujiang New Town Station
Tickets: 380 to 1380 yuan via the venue’s website or Damai
2. Zhou Shen’s Concert Tour: Shenzhen

From China’s Hunan province, Zhou will perform in Shenzhen as part of his tour called “ShenShen”, which sounds like the word for “deeply” in Mandarin.
The tour, which began in Shanghai in May, coincides with the 10th anniversary of his first studio single, “The Rose and the Deer” (2015).
The concerts feature 3D visual effects, creating an “emotional universe” to accompany his four-octave range.
After growing up in central and southwestern China, Zhou studied Bel canto at the Lviv National Music Academy in Ukraine. In 2014, at the age of 21, he took part in the third season of The Voice of China, a singing competition show, where he garnered national acclaim.
Dates: December 6 and 7
Venue: Shenzhen Universiade Sports Centre
How to get there: Shenzhen Metro Line 3 to Dayun Centre
Tickets: 399 to 1,699 yuan via Damai, Piaoxingqiu or Ctrip
3. The Vienna Berlin Music Club – Philharmonix in Concert: Shenzhen

Comprising seven musicians, including three from the Vienna Philharmonic and two from the Berlin Philharmonic orchestras, Philharmonix will play in Shenzhen to kick off the China leg of its East Asia tour.
Calling itself “the Vienna Berlin Music Club”, the group stands out among other classical music repertoires because of its fun-loving approach to performance, as the members creatively mix in sounds from other genres such as folk, jazz, Latin and pop.
Date: December 11
Venue: Symphony Hall, Shenzhen Concert Hall
How to get there: Shenzhen Metro Line 4 to Children’s Palace Station
Tickets: 80 to 580 yuan via the venue’s website
4. Borodin Quartet 80th Anniversary Concerts: Shostakovich Complete String Quartets

The Borodin Quartet will be in Guangzhou to perform the complete quartet cycle of its one-time close collaborator, Russian composer Dmitri Shostakovich, 50 years after his death.
Founded in Moscow in 1945, the Borodin Quartet is among the world’s oldest and most celebrated string quartets, one that has a long history of performing in China.
This stop on the quartet’s 80th-anniversary tour marks its fifth appearance at the Xinghai Concert Hall.
Dates: December 20, 21, 23, 24 and 26
Venue: Xinghai Concert Hall
How to get there: Guangzhou Metro Line 6 to Shaheding Station
Tickets: 180 to 380 yuan via the venue’s website
5. Sandy Lam ‘Resonance’ 2025 Concert Tour: Shenzhen

On December 31, Sandy Lam Yik-lin will perform a New Year’s Eve concert in Shenzhen. It is a full-circle moment for her “Resonance” tour, which began in the same stadium in February.
Marking 40 years since the 1985 release of her self-titled debut album, the Hong Kong songstress has resumed touring in the past year after a long hiatus, proving her enduring popularity by filling stadiums in nearly 30 cities across mainland China and Macau.
Date: December 31
Venue: Shenzhen Bay Sports Centre
How to get there: Shenzhen Metro Line 11 to Houhai Station
Tickets: TBA

data.title: Europe looks for common ground with Indo-Pacific nations bruised by US-China rivalry
data.description:
Dozens of senior officials and diplomats from across Asia, Africa and the Pacific will touch down in Brussels on Thursday, as the European Union tries to convince them it is a more reliable partner than the United States and China.
More than 70 delegations will attend the bloc’s fourth Indo-Pacific forum, with more than 50 ministers or vice-ministers expected at talks on Friday, EU officials said. Crucially, neither Beijing or Washington are invited.
“There was no invitation to special guests – this is only for our Indo-Pacific partners. It is an occasion to discuss with them in an open and frank way. With the US, Canada, Norway and others, we already discuss with them the Indo-Pacific,” said one official involved in the planning, referring to countries previously invited to the forum.
A second said that “China has never been invited – it’s not about China”, even if the discussion was likely to turn to Beijing’s stronghold on critical minerals.
“China has not attended in the past but this doesn’t mean the principle of the forum is against anyone. We have a lot of dedicated and high-level channels with China … dialogue with China has its own momentum,” they added.

With many countries involved battered by US President Donald Trump’s trade tariffs and bruised from being caught in the crosshairs of the superpower rivalry, organisers are hopeful that this forum might be more fruitful than previous editions.
In previous years, the EU has struggled even to get its own people to attend. Two years ago in Stockholm, barely half the bloc’s 27 foreign ministers showed up, despite the forum being bolted onto the end of a meeting that almost all of them attended. Organisers declined to say how many would be there this time around.
Last year in Belgium, meanwhile, there was a slanging match over “double standards”, when ministers from countries such as Indonesia and Sri Lanka accused their hosts of failing to apply the same rigorous critique to Israel’s bombardment of Gaza that it does to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.
“I appeal to all of you to listen to your heart and do the right thing, stop the atrocities in Gaza, in Palestine,” then-Indonesian foreign minister Retno Marsudi told the forum, adding that “consistency with values and actions reflect sincerity of moral high ground … it shows who we actually are”.
But this time around, observers and officials sense an opening for Europe: Trump’s tariffs and Beijing’s export controls have set off a global scramble for diversification. This could be advanced by Trump’s decision to boycott the first G20 summit to be held in Africa this weekend.
The US has threatened to punish South Africa if it attempts to arrange a leaders’ statement in its absence and EU officials involved in the planning described a vacuum that was unfilled. Surprisingly, they said, Beijing had not attempted to move into the space.
Accordingly, there are no plans for EU leaders to meet Chinese Premier Li Qiang in South Africa, with officials saying there was no point meeting until some of the concerns raised by European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen with Li at the UN General Assembly in September or by European Council chief Antonio Costa at the Asean summit in Kuala Lumpur in October are addressed.
Instead, sources said, the Europeans were rallying around hosts South Africa in a bid to make the summit a success.

On the Indo-Pacific front, the EU hopes to soon conclude free-trade deals with several attendees, including India and Indonesia, while plans are afoot to upgrade its ties with the Asean members to a “comprehensive, strategic partnership” on the 50th anniversary of relations in 2027.
A survey this year by the ISEAS-Yusof Ishak Institute – a think tank – found that for Asean members, the EU is the preferred hedging partner in the superpower rivalry, taking 36.3 per cent of the vote, even as Europe’s reputation continues to suffer over the Gaza issue.
Under the new Indonesian presidency of Prabowo Subianto, the “tone has changed”, said Dewi Fortuna Anwar, a political scientist and chair of the Jakarta-based Habibie Centre. Before he took office, Prabowo regularly bashed Europe, but Trump had thrust them closer together, she said.
“Throughout his career and throughout his campaign, Prabowo pointed out that the EU has no moral standing on human rights and on Asia due to its colonial past and silence and complicity of Israel’s war in Gaza,” Anwar said.
“During his foreign policy speech before he was elected president, he took a question from an EU person in the audience, and told them Indonesia doesn’t need Europe. But then he switched completely. I think we have to be thankful to Trump … the EU and Indonesia now need each other. The tone has changed completely.”
The summit’s success, however, may depend on whether the hosts can leave some of their core issues at the door, including Ukraine.
Two years ago, some attendees were vexed when Ukraine’s then-foreign minister Dmytro Kuleba showed up unannounced. One visiting official described it at the time to the Post as “an ambush”. Another said they had “not come for a lecture”.
It will also provide a test for Kaja Kallas, the bloc’s top diplomat who will chair Thursday’s dinner and Friday’s talks, and for whom Russia is clearly the No 1 foreign policy issue. To the extent that Kallas has focused on Asia during her one-year tenure, it has largely been through the lens of the war.
At an event in Brussels on Wednesday, Kallas said that without China’s support, Russia would have already lost the war and warned that Europe’s economic entanglement with Beijing was constraining its ability to punish Moscow.
China “might also hurt you, and that’s the problem”, said Kallas, according to Bloomberg. “If you’re not willing to pay the price that they are going to impose on you, then it’s hard to act,” she added.
Moreover, a recent discussion of the EU’s foreign ministers on the Indo-Pacific – chaired by Kallas – lasted just nine minutes.

European experts are sceptical as to whether the event in Brussels will deliver much. Stefania Benaglia, an independent EU foreign policy adviser, said the bloc was on a steep learning curve.
“We’re learning how to do foreign policy – but not strategic foreign policy. It mostly happens as a reaction to events,” Benaglia said.
“I have low expectations – this is the first summit with a new [high representative] who doesn’t have Indo-Pacific high up in her priorities. So it will be a little bit of getting to know each other,” she said.
“I would not expect ground-shaking outcomes – there is not the time or this is not the format for that. Instead the fact that the meeting is taking place, the discussions have not been discontinued – that is the outcome.”

data.title: Helmsman’s phone distraction blamed for South Korean ferry grounding
data.description:
A South Korean ferry ran aground after the helmsman became distracted by his mobile phone, a member of the coastguard said on Thursday.
All 267 passengers and crew were safely brought to a nearby port after the ferry crashed on an uninhabited island off South Korea’s southwestern coast.
The passenger vessel was travelling from Jeju Island to the port city of Mokpo when it ran aground after 8pm (local time) on Wednesday, authorities said.
A preliminary investigation found that “the officer responsible for steering had been looking at his mobile phone and allowed autopilot to take control in an area where the ship should have been manually operated”, a Korea coastguard official said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
“As a result, the vessel missed the proper moment to change course, veered towards the uninhabited island and ran aground.”
The coastguard plans to press criminal charges against the individual as it was “clear negligence”, the official added.
No serious injuries were reported, and 246 passengers were safely moved to a nearby port on rescue ships, the Ministry of Safety said in a statement.

According to the authorities, 21 crew members remained on the vessel, which was later brought to Mokpo’s port around 5.44am.
Among the passengers, 27 people were taken to a hospital due to light injuries, including headaches and back pain.
Coastguard Commissioner Kim Yong-jin had earlier told reporters that a preliminary assessment suggested “errors” by the crew may have caused the accident.
A photo released by Yonhap News Agency showed the bow of the ferry pressed up against the island’s tree-covered shore.
Another image showed passengers waiting to be rescued, many of them wearing what appear to be life vests.
President Lee Jae-myung had ordered “swift rescue efforts” and instructed authorities to provide real-time updates to the public, his office said.
In 2014, a ferry carrying more than 470 passengers – mostly pupils on a school trip – capsized off the southwestern coast, killing 304 people in South Korea’s worst maritime disaster.
The salvaged wreck of the Sewol ferry was brought to Mokpo nearly three years later.

data.title: Rare stingray in Victoria Harbour a welcome sign of nature’s restoration
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
The November 12 report of an endangered eagle ray fished from Victoria Harbor is a testament to Hong Kong’s strident efforts at marine conservation. It is reminiscent of colonial newspapers a century ago reporting all sorts of marine wildlife in the harbour, including abundant fish, even large rays and sharks.
It is hard for us now to imagine Victoria Harbor teeming with marine life. This is due to what is known as the “shifting baseline syndrome”, wherein the lack of collective living memory makes us convinced that the current reality is the only one that has ever existed.
Yet, our marine life is rebounding. Since 2013, Hong Kong has implemented a complete ban on benthic trawling, a destructive fishing practice that drags nets with chains across the seabed to catch, well, everything, destroying seabed habitats including corals, sponges and shellfish beds in the process. Benthic trawling continues to plague the oceans, but at least Hong Kong’s coastal areas are at long last enjoying some reprieve.
The mandatory reduction of fishing pressure offers a rare opportunity to observe the “rewilding” of our local seas. Even in the early days of the trawl ban, the abundance and variety of wildlife sampled from the sea floor began to change – at that time observed by Professor Kenny Leung’s team at the Swire Institute of Marine Science.
My own observations were equally hopeful. A year after the ban, I began to see a great quantity of commercially important swimming crabs on many dives – animals that would normally be captured by trawling. Some time later we witnessed hundreds of juvenile butterfly rays sprinting about Centre Island in Tolo Harbour. After that I encountered a large aggregation of adult butterfly rays, nearly 2 metres in width and pregnant, resting in the sand just off the beach at Cheung Chau.
These anecdotal observations are the positive signs of nature’s restoration, and by extension, the restoration of the ecosystem services we value. Small species of fish and shellfish will quickly repopulate, and their predators – like stingrays – will take longer to re-establish local populations and grow to reproductive size. Someday soon we may also start to observe the return of stingray predators, including hammerhead sharks.
As our city evolves new policies towards promoting Hong Kong’s extensive natural beauty and outstanding biodiversity via eco-tourism, I can think of no better news. A fisher’s tale of catching a rare stingray is a positive story to share, indeed – provided both fisher and fish enjoyed the catch, and release, respectively.
Dr David M. Baker, professor and interim director of the Swire Institute of Marine Science, University of Hong Kong
Tai Po green belt site should not be used for housing
I plead with the Hong Kong government to stop treating green belts as land banks and to stop granting development rights to lots where development should not take place. With the United Nations Climate Conference (Cop30) in Belem, Brazil now under way emphasising the need to save forests in the interests of mitigating climate change, why does the government consider such areas suitable for development?
Under the Land Sharing Pilot Scheme, the government has granted in-principle approval of an application to develop the green belt site at Lo Fai Road and Ting Kok Road in Tai Po for housing. This will necessitate the felling of hundreds of trees.
This development is unnecessary as the planned Northern Metropolis will provide all the additional housing Hong Kong needs, although this project too needs to be carefully planned and implemented to mitigate the environmental impact on the Ramsar Convention site of Mai Po.
The Tai Po proposal will cause considerable environmental and traffic problems during site formation and when the development is completed. The access road is situated halfway between a dangerous downhill bend and the traffic lights at the road junction – how this can be safe, I do not know.
I will be interested to see if the various impact assessments carried out by the developer prove to be reliable.
Allan Hay, Tai Po
In Central Asia, Russia’s influence is not so easily eroded
I refer to the article, “As US and China turn their eyes to Central Asia, Russia loses out” (November 18).
As a Russian, I know my country will never lose out in that region because neither the US nor China is really interested in it since their shops don’t stock en masse consumer goods made in Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan and Uzbekistan.
Most of these countries being landlocked is something of a handicap. As are all of them speaking Russian and not English or Mandarin. Also, the strictures imposed on us by the West over Ukraine keep our companies there, helping us bypass the restrictions at full stretch.
Actually, both the US and China had a lot of time to check out the land in the region. So both know it’s an absolute piffle to expect a military or economic encroachment on Russia there like that in Eastern Europe after the collapse of the Soviet Union in 1991.
Mergen Mongush, Moscow

data.title: Hong Kong firefighters rescue boy, 14, from smoke-filled Kwai Chung flat
data.description:
A 14-year-old boy has been rescued after heavy smoke filled a public housing block in Hong Kong’s Kwai Chung, prompting the evacuation of more than 50 residents.
Police said they had received multiple reports since 7.41am on Thursday of a suspected fire at Block 8 of the Kwai Shing West Estate.
Firefighters rescued the boy from a flat. He was believed to have inhaled dense smoke and was taken to the Princess Margaret Hospital in Lai Chi Kok for treatment.
More than 50 residents self-evacuated from the building.
The force said that a preliminary investigation by firefighters found no evidence of a fire, adding that nothing suspicious was discovered in the case.

data.title: Nasa shares new images of 3I/ATLAS and rejects alien spacecraft ‘rumours’
data.description:
Nasa released fresh images on Wednesday of the interstellar object called 3I/ATLAS that astronomers have determined is a comet probably even older than our solar system, as US space agency officials dismissed speculation that it was actually an alien spacecraft.
3I/ATLAS was first spotted in July by an Asteroid Terrestrial-impact Last Alert System, or ATLAS, telescope located in Rio Hurtado, Chile, and has been tracked by astronomers since then. Its unusual trajectory indicated that it was passing through our solar system from parts unknown.
“It’s natural to wonder what it is. We love that the world wondered along with us,” Nicola Fox, associate administrator of Nasa’s Science Mission Directorate, told a news briefing in Greenbelt, Maryland, referring to the comet as “our friendly solar system visitor”.
“We were quick to be able to say, ‘Yup, it definitely behaves like a comet. We certainly haven’t seen any technosignatures or anything from it that would lead us to believe it was anything other than a comet.”

We've just released the latest images of the interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS, as observed by eight different spacecraft, satellites, and telescopes.
Here's what we've learned about the comet — and how we're studying it across the solar system: https://t.co/ZIt1Qq6DSp pic.twitter.com/ITD6BqVlGn
— NASA (@NASA) November 19, 2025

3I/ATLAS is only the third interstellar object ever observed by astronomers travelling through the solar system. The others were comets called 1I/‘Oumuamua, detected in 2017, and 2I/Borisov, discovered in 2019.
Comets are small solid celestial bodies that are a combination of rocky and icy material that evaporates as they warm when getting close to a star like our sun.
3I/ATLAS has attracted particular attention because of one scientist’s suggestion that it is not a comet but rather alien technology due to its trajectory, composition and other factors.
At the outset of Wednesday’s briefing, Nasa Associate Administrator Amit Kshatriya said, “To start with, I’d like to address the rumours” about the nature of 3I/ATLAS.
“I think it’s important that we talk about that. This object is a comet,” Kshatriya said. “It looks and behaves like a comet. And all evidence points to it being a comet.”
Kshatriya noted that Nasa missions were actively searching for signs of possible life beyond Earth, pointing to research published in September showing that a sample obtained by Nasa’s Perseverance rover of rock formed billions of years ago from sediment on the bottom of a lake contains potential signs of ancient microbial life on Mars.


“We want very much to find signs of life in the universe,” Kshatriya said.
Nasa said 3I/ATLAS posed no threat to Earth and would get no closer than about 275 million km (170 million miles) to our planet.
Fox said Nasa has studied 3I/ATLAS with more than a dozen scientific platforms including the Hubble and James Webb space telescopes and satellites orbiting Mars.
The new Nasa images, all taken from afar, showed it with a blurry appearance but with the clear presence of a telltale coma - the hazy cloud of gas and dust around its nucleus - and the tail of dust following its orbit. The release of the images had been delayed by the US government shutdown.
Tom Statler, lead Nasa scientist for solar system small bodies, said the size of the comet’s nucleus was hard to pin down, but estimated it based on Hubble observations to be in the range of “a couple of thousand feet to a couple of miles” in diameter. Statler said the nucleus seems to be “not very far from being round”.
While the comet’s precise point of origin remained unclear, the Nasa scientists said they believe it hailed from a solar system older than our own, which formed about 4.5 billion years ago. The scientists also said its composition differed in some ways from comets from our own solar system, as might be expected because it formed in a solar system that may have a different makeup than ours.

Astronomers around the world have intensively studied 3I/ATLAS.
University of Oxford astrophysicist Chris Lintott said: “So far, it seems to be made of the same sort of stuff we see for comets in our solar system - plenty of carbon dioxide and some water, carbon monoxide and other such molecules. We’ve also seen cyanide - normal for a comet - and lots of nickel, which is a bit surprising but not too unprecedented. We saw similar things in a previous interstellar comet, 2I/Borisov, and in some solar system comets.
“The idea that 3I/ATLAS could be an alien spacecraft is simply nonsense. There’s nothing about it that suggests such a thing, and you might as well argue that the moon is made of cheese,” Lintott said.
3I/ATLAS was on its way out of the solar system, according to University of Hawaii astronomer Larry Denneau, co-principal investigator for ATLAS. Its closest approach to the sun came in October, and its closest approach to the Earth will come in about a month, Denneau said.

data.title: CICC to absorb 2 smaller rivals to create US$140 billion brokerage
data.description:
China International Capital Corp (CICC) plans to absorb two smaller brokerages to create a new entity worth 1 trillion yuan (US$140 billion) in assets, as a state-led consolidation gathers pace to meet Beijing’s goal of creating investment banks that can compete with the likes of Goldman Sachs.
CICC would take over Dongxing Securities and Cinda Securities through stock swaps with the shareholders of the two peers, it said in an exchange statement on Wednesday night, without giving details. Shares of the three companies will be suspended from trading on the Shanghai exchange for up to 25 days.
The consolidation marks the latest move by China’s securities industry to fulfil President Xi Jinping’s ambition of building financial giants amid an all-out confrontation with the US that threatens financial decoupling. CICC, Dongxing and Cinda are controlled by Central Huijin Investment, a unit of China’s sovereign wealth fund, which should result in a smooth restructuring.
“The restructuring is conducive to building a first-class investment [bank] and supporting the reform of the financial market and the high-quality development of the securities industry,” CICC said in the statement.
The revamp would consolidate and complement the companies’ resources and strengths, which in turn would better serve the state’s strategy and the economy, and boost shareholder returns, according to the statement.
The merged brokerage’s assets of 1 trillion yuan would rank it fourth after Citic Securities, Guotai Haitong Securities and Huatai Securities, according to data provider Wind Information.
CICC had total assets of 764.9 billion yuan at the end of the third quarter, Dongxing had 116.3 billion yuan and Cinda had 128.2 billion yuan, according to their quarterly reports.
Central Huijin has a 40 per cent stake in CICC, and controls a 45 per cent interest in Dongxing and a 79 per cent stake in Cinda through its units. The investment unit is also a major shareholder of China Galaxy Securities, Shenwan Hongyuan Group and Great Wall Glory Securities.
